personality_belief.metaphysical_profile: Used only as cultural flavor, never as a decision rule.
personality_belief.decision_style: practical and constrained by real workflow trade-offs
personality_belief.trust_orientation: needs proof from concrete workflows, not just positioning
personality_belief.self_image: responsible, busy, and careful about adding complexity

economic_profile.purchase_decision_process: tests fit against current workflow before committing

life_goals: stay on top of work without constant catch-up
fears: Paying for another tool that becomes shelfware
Creating a workflow that only Jordan can maintain
Looking careless with client or colleague information
Adding one more place to check at the end of a long day
aspirations: Reduce admin drag without losing control
Look organised and dependable under pressure
Keep enough energy for judgment-heavy work instead of chasing loose ends
Make it easier to step away from work without worrying something important was missed
moral_boundaries: will not trade away other people's privacy for convenience
status_concerns: does not want to appear disorganised in front of clients or team
practical_priorities: Keep commitments visible across work and home
Avoid tools that create a second inbox or extra maintenance burden
Protect short blocks of focus time from being consumed by follow-up
Spend only when the workflow benefit is obvious inside normal weekly pressure
tradeoff_rules: Will tolerate a short setup if it replaces repeated manual chasing within two weeks
Prefers flexible monthly spend over annual lock-in because cash flow can change quickly
Chooses simpler workflows over feature depth when both can solve the same job
Will not trade privacy or explainability for convenience
non_negotiables: Easy exit if the tool does not fit
No pressure to upload more data than necessary

life_story.childhood_background: Grew up in a household that valued practical decisions and financial caution in a hong kong smb context.
life_story.education_journey: Completed postgraduate level education and learned through a mix of formal training and on-the-job repetition during a mature operator phase.
life_story.career_path: Jordan moved into operations because they are good at spotting where handoffs fail, writing a process down clearly, and keeping promises visible. Their postgraduate education shows up less as theory and more as a habit of documenting decisions and comparing options before committing.
life_story.family_story: Currently single parent with one child, which shapes both available time and risk tolerance. Caregiving load is high.
life_story.current_daily_routine: Most weekdays are split between coordination work, message follow-up, and protecting small windows of focused time. Schedule pressure is high, decision speed is deliberate, and purchase authority is workflow tool evaluator.
recent_life_events: trying to reduce context-switching during the week
reassessing workflow due to high cash-flow volatility
frustrations: family responsibilities make admin work feel heavier
short-term cash pressure makes software decisions feel riskier
hidden_needs: Confidence that a tool will simplify the week quickly, not eventually
A way to trial real value without exposing too much sensitive data
Evidence that teammates can follow the workflow without relying on Jordan to translate it
life_story.summary: Jordan Wong is a 55-year-old non-binary operations manager in Hong Kong, raising one child as a single parent in a two-person household. They are bilingual in Cantonese and English and are known for keeping scattered work moving even when the week changes shape.
life_story.family_context: Caregiving is not abstract in Jordan's week. School, household coordination, and being the default responsible adult compress available time, so even small inefficiencies at work feel expensive at home.
life_story.daily_routine: A typical weekday starts with scanning messages, calendars, and task lists across devices to see what changed overnight. Work hours are filled with coordination, follow-up, exceptions, and small approvals. Focus time comes in short windows, so Jordan prefers tools that help them resume context quickly on phone and laptop without a long re-entry cost.
recent_pressures: Software spend has been reviewed more closely because cash flow feels less predictable
The team has accumulated too many parallel channels for updates and follow-up
Jordan is trying to reduce end-of-day cleanup work that spills into family time
coping_patterns: Documents processes so work can survive interruptions
Uses manual checklists as a backstop even when they are tedious
Delays adopting new tools until a concrete workflow can be tested
Keeps subscriptions under review instead of assuming they should renew

behavior_profile.buying_behavior: Jordan is open to change when the path is concrete, but skepticism rises fast when setup looks messy or benefits stay abstract.
emotional_triggers: feeling more in control of follow-up
evaluation_habits: Starts with peer signal or a credible example from a similar workflow
Looks for a short before-and-after demonstration rather than a broad feature tour
Tests whether the tool reduces follow-up work in an ordinary busy week, not in an ideal demo scenario
Reads pricing and privacy details early to avoid wasting evaluation time
workflow_habits: Maintains documented processes even when the actual execution still depends on spreadsheets, reminders, and message search
Uses different devices for different moments: phone for triage, laptop for structured work, and whichever device is nearby for quick checks
Keeps a manual fallback until the new workflow has survived real interruptions
communication_habits: Direct in meetings and written feedback
Prefers plain language over marketing phrasing
Will share a useful tool with peers only after it has proven reliable in practice
tool_attitude: High digital literacy does not translate into patience for unnecessary complexity
Moderately open to automation if it stays reviewable and easy to correct
High app fatigue makes another dashboard a harder sell than a workflow improvement inside existing habits
trust_signals: Clear onboarding that respects limited time
Specific stories from similar teams or operators
Visible privacy stance and minimal-data workflow options
A trial that produces one useful result quickly
blockers: Unclear data retention or model training policy
Anything that creates extra explanation work for internal approval
adoption_momentum: Low technical switching cost helps if initial proof is strong
Manager review still matters before wider rollout or paid commitment

active_pain_points: Family responsibilities make admin work feel heavier
Short-term cash pressure makes software decisions feel riskier
latent_pain_points: mental load from tracking too many loose ends
slow drift between promises and actual follow-up
primary_jobs: Avoid dropped tasks when context is spread across channels
Keep commitments visible without building a heavy project-management layer
underlying_tensions: The current system is flexible enough to survive daily chaos but too loose to prevent drift
Because switching cost is structurally low, Jordan can leave a tool easily, but that also makes them cautious about starting habits that may not last
Documented workflows exist, yet actual execution still depends on memory and message recovery at busy moments
current_workarounds: Using existing messaging apps to keep work moving when formal systems are slower
failure_modes: A follow-up gets delayed because the promise lives in a chat thread rather than a task view
Jordan spends evening time reconstructing context from messages
A tool looks efficient in demo mode but adds maintenance work once real exceptions appear
Privacy concerns slow adoption because nobody can explain clearly what data is required
desired_outcomes: See what needs follow-up without hunting across channels
Keep the workflow simple enough for others to adopt
Try a new system without committing sensitive information too early
trial_requirements: A short visible trial using a realistic workflow
Minimal setup and minimal sensitive data
Clear proof of how the tool handles exceptions and handoffs
Enough evidence to survive comparison with current spreadsheets and messaging habits

sensitive_reality_layer.social_risk_profile: does not want to look careless with colleagues' or clients' information
sensitive_reality_layer.fairness_and_inclusion_profile: expects product language to remain respectful and not assume one type of user
response_boundaries: will not share private third-party data without a clear reason
sensitive_reality_layer.privacy_posture: Jordan defaults to data minimisation. They are not anti-technology, but they want to know exactly why data is needed, where it goes, and how easy it is to remove later.
sensitive_reality_layer.inclusion_expectations: Product language should feel respectful, practical, and not built around a single assumed user identity or household model.
disclosure_boundaries: Will not share private third-party data without a clear operational reason
Avoids exposing client or colleague details in early trials
Does not want personal identity context treated as the explanation for every preference
sensitive_reality_layer.social_risk: Jordan is sensitive to looking careless, disorganised, or naive in front of colleagues, clients, and managers. Trust drops if a product makes them feel they have to defend it socially before it proves itself operationally.
language_preferences: Can operate comfortably in Cantonese or English, but clarity matters more than tone polish
discomfort_triggers: Pushy requests for full data access too early
Identity labeling that feels performative or irrelevant to the task
Claims that imply automation can be trusted without human checking
reassurance_needs: Clear boundaries on model training and retention
Evidence that the product can fit a mixed-device, interruption-heavy routine

audit_evidence_layer.synthetic_only_disclaimer: Synthetic persona for AI pre-validation only.
